coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous determination of insulin and glucose in human serum based on dual emissive fluorescent nano-aptasensor of carbon dots and CdTe/CdS/ZnS quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Muhammad, Sohail
Xu, Guanhong
Wei, Fangdi
Cen, Yao
Younis, Muhammad Rizwan
Xu, Xiaoman
Shi, Menglan
Cheng, Xia
Chai, Yuying
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: A dual emissive fluorescent nano aptasensor was designed for simultaneous detection of human serum glucose and insulin levels. CdTe/CdS/ZnS quantum dots and carbon dots were synthesized and conjugated with the aptamers of glucose and insulin, respectively. The fluorescence of these conjugates was quenched by nano-graphite based on the static quenching and inner filter effect, and restored on the addition of the analytes. All reaction parameters were optimized using analysis of variance, and a polynomial regression model was constructed and verified to explain the effects of reaction parameters on the outcome. The linear ranges of insulin and glucose were 0.2–2 nM and 0.5–7 mM, respectively. The limits of detection for insulin and glucose were 0.018 nM and 0.058 mM, respectively. All the values of relative error % were within the range of −5.00−4.00%, and the values of relative standard deviation % were ≤3.77%. The changes in fluorescence intensity restoration of the probe in the presence of various interfering species were less than 5%, and all the recoveries for glucose and insulin were in the range of 90–110%. The probe was validated and applied successfully to the determination of the stated analytes in human serum. The fabricated aptasensor can be used to diagnose diabetes and its type with a single assay in clinical diagnostic labs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Carbon dots
Aptasensor
Glucose
Insulin
Diabetes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CdTe/CdS/ZnS QDs and CDs
Fabrication of dual emissive nano aptasensor
Statistical Modeling: a multiple polynomial regression model

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of CDs and QDs (a), UV–vis spectra of CDs and QDs, and CDs and QDs linked with aptamers (b), fluorescence spectra of physical mixture of CDs, QDs and aptamers, and dual emissive probe (c), agarose gel electrophoresis image of (i) glucose aptamer, (ii) physical mixture of QDs and glucose aptamer, (iii) complex of QDs and aptamer, (iv) insulin aptamer, (v) physical mixture of CDs and insulin aptamer, and (vi) complex of CDs and insulin aptamer (d).

FL quenching of QDs-Apt (a) and CDs-Apt (b) by different concentrations of NG at 20 °C and 35 °C, absorption spectrum of the NG and emission spectrum of the probe (c), fluorescence lifetime spectra of CDs-Apt and QDs-Apt in the absence and presence of NG (d).
Profile plot of interaction effect of QDs concentration and incubation time.
Observed, predicted and standard residual plots of QDs’ FLI restoration %.

Restoration of FL intensities of CDs-apt (A) and QDs-apt (B) on addition of different concentrations of insulin (a–h) and glucose (i–p). Insulin concentrations (nM) are; (a) 0, (b) 0.2, (c) 0.4, (d) 0.8, (e) 1.2, (f) 1.6, (g) 1.8, and (h) 2.0, while glucose concentrations (mM) are, (i) 0, (j) 0.5, (k) 1.0, (l) 2.0, (m) 4.0, (n) 5.0, (o) 6.0, and (p) 7.0.
Schematic illustration of the probe design and its working principle.
Accuracy and precision of aptasensor for the determination of serum insulin and glucose levels.
Fluctuation in relative FLI% on addition of various interfering species.
Determination of insulin and glucose concentrations in human serum samples.
Comparison between the built method and gold standard method.
Comparison of dual emissive probe features with reported methods.

row.entry: Glucose and insulin solutions, human serum

head.ce:title: Simultaneous determination of insulin and glucose in human serum based on dual emissive fluorescent nano-aptasensor of carbon dots and CdTe/CdS/ZnS quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Sciences, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Sciences
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual emissive fluorescent nano aptasensor was designed for simultaneous detection of human serum glucose and insulin levels. CdTe/CdS/ZnS quantum dots and carbon dots were synthesized and conjugated with the aptamers of glucose and insulin, respectively. The fluorescence of these conjugates was quenched by nano-graphite based on the static quenching and inner filter effect, and restored on the addition of the analytes. All reaction parameters were optimized using analysis of variance, and a polynomial regression model was constructed and verified to explain the effects of reaction parameters on the outcome. The linear ranges of insulin and glucose were 0.2–2 nM and 0.5–7 mM, respectively. The limits of detection for insulin and glucose were 0.018 nM and 0.058 mM, respectively. All the values of relative error % were within the range of −5.00−4.00%, and the values of relative standard deviation % were ≤3.77%. The changes in fluorescence intensity restoration of the probe in the presence of various interfering species were less than 5%, and all the recoveries for glucose and insulin were in the range of 90–110%. The probe was validated and applied successfully to the determination of the stated analytes in human serum. The fabricated aptasensor can be used to diagnose diabetes and its type with a single assay in clinical diagnostic labs.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus (DM) is characterized by hyperglycemic complex metabolic disorders with a high risk of macro and microvascular and non-vascular diseases [1]. These complications include cancer, liver and kidney diseases, infections, blindness, mental, neurological and cardiovascular disorders. There are two major categories of DM. Type 1 DM is characterized by absolute and severe deficiency or lack of insulin caused by autoimmune destruction of β cells of islets of Langerhans, and type 2 DM is associated with impairment in insulin function [1–3]. Determination of serum glucose level helps to diagnose DM as normal reference range of fasting serum glucose level is 3.9–5.6 mM [4,5] while serum insulin level measurement is considered as differential diagnostic tool of type 1 DM and type 2 DM as normal reference range of fasting serum insulin is 0.012–173 nM [6–8]. Moreover, insulin concentration in human serum is a potential factor to assess β cells’ function of type 1 DM patients who have had a transplantation of islet [2]. So, it is significant to determine the glucose and insulin levels in human serum in diagnosing and treating DM.

Various analytical procedures are available for the determination of serum insulin level, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [9], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [10], electrochemical methods [11] and fluorescence-based techniques [10,12,13], etc. Similarly, a pool of techniques is also available for measurement of serum glucose concentration, e.g. surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [14], colorimetry [15–18], electrochemical method [19] and fluorescence-based assay [20,21], etc. Simultaneous determination of glucose and insulin is of great worth concerning time and sample volume, as a single assay can diagnose DM and its type. The enzyme-based techniques are available for this purpose [22]. The major drawback of using enzymes in analytical techniques is their labile nature with respect to procedure variables like temperature, pH, etc.

Fluorescent nanoparticles are gaining popularity in the fields of analytical chemistry, bioimaging, targeted delivery due to their peculiar tunable optical behavior and stability [23–25]. Use of aptamers, namely single-stranded sequence of DNA or RNA, along with the fluorescent nanoparticles, like carbon dots (CDs) and quantum dots (QDs), is presenting specificity and sensitivity comparable to the antibody-based assays but remarkable stability is there in case of aptamer-based analytical techniques [26,27]. Moreover, aptamers are non-immunogenic, easy to prepare and handle, as well as production cost is low [28]. Because of these properties, fluorescent aptamer nanosensors to quantify biomolecules, biomarkers and drugs in the body and commercially available preparations, have become hot areas of research in recent years, which are replacing enzyme and antibody-based assays [29,30].

Herein, a dual emissive fluorescent nano aptasensor for simultaneous determination of insulin and glucose concentrations in human serum was designed. Furthermore, the statistical model explaining the effects of multilevel multiple reaction parameters on a single outcome was constructed.

C4H6CdO4⋅2H2O (98.5%), Na2S⋅9H2O (98%), Na2HPO4⋅12H2O, Citric acid monohydrate, ZnSO4⋅7H2O (99.5%), NaBH4 (96%), diethylenetriamine, 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), bovine serum albumin (BSA), glucose and powdered tellurium were purchased from Sinopharm Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Mercaptopropanoic acid (MPA, 99%) was from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Nano-graphite (NG, 5–15 nm) was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co. Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Recombinant human Insulin was from Beyon Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). The manufacturer of DNA oligonucleotides was Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequences of DNA oligonucleotides bases were as follows.
Insulin aptamer: 5 ′ -NH2-(CH 2)6-GGTGGTGGGGGGGGTTGGTAGGGTGTCTTC-3′ [6]
Glucose aptamer: 5 ′ -NH2-(CH 2)6-AGCTACAGCTAC-3′ [31]
The purified water used in the experiment was prepared using the Direct-Q water purification system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA). All other reagents used were of analytical grade.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a resolution of 2 cm–1. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a UV2450 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

A HIT-ACHI F-4600 ﬂuorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) with 1 cm quartz cell was used to record photoluminescence spectra and FL intensity. Excitation and emission slits with 5 nm widths, excitation wavelength of 360 nm, emission wavelengths of 450 nm and 590 nm, scan speed of 1200 nm/min and voltage 1000 v were used to operate the equipment. Gel electrophoresis apparatus (Tanon HE-120, Shanghai, china) was used to confirm the successful binding of aptamers on the surfaces of CDs and QDs, respectively. FLS980 spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, UK) was used for fluorescence lifetime measurements.
IBM SPSS 20, graph-pad prism 5, design expert 11, MS excel 13, Origin lab pro 8.5 and endnote X7 were used to analyze and manage the experimental data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CdTe/CdS/ZnS QDs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation and characterization of QDs were finished using an already established method by our research group [32]. Briefly, QDs’ core, namely CdTe, was prepared by adding freshly prepared NaHTe solution to the mixture of MPA and aqueous solution of Cd2+ saturated with nitrogen at pH 10. Then, the mixture was refluxed at 90 °C for 8 h. Concentrations of Cd2+, Te and MPA were 2 mM, 0.5 mM and 4.8 mM, respectively. At the next step, 20 mL aqueous solution of ZnSO4.7H2O, C4H6CdO4.2H2O and MPA was prepared and its pH was adjusted to 10.5 ± 0.5 using 1 M NaOH aqueous solution. This solution was named as the precursor solution. The sulfur solution was prepared by dissolving Na2S.9H2O in water. Finally, 20 mL of QDs’ core solution was added to a three-necked glass flask and saturated with nitrogen. 20 mL of the precursor solution was added to the nitrogen-saturated QDs’ core solution and stirred for 30 min. 5 mL of sulfur solution was added to the prepared mixture and refluxed at 90 °C for 3 h. Concentration of CdTe QDs was 0.25 mM while molar ratio of MPA:S2−:Cd2+:Zn2+ was 2:2:1:2. The solution was evaporated to 1/4th of its original volume using a rotary evaporator. QDs were precipitated by ethanol and separated by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The product was vacuum dried at 40 °C and then characterized.

CDs were prepared and characterized by following a reported method [33]. In short, 1.2 g of citric acid monohydrate and 600 μL of diethylene triamine were added to 20 mL of water. The mixture was poured to a 30 mL stainless steel autoclave equipped with Teflon and experienced through hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 h. The product was concentrated by a rotary evaporator, purified thrice with acetone, vacuum dried and then characterized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of dual emissive nano aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Carbodiimide chemistry was used to prepare conjugates of QDs and CDs with aptamers [34,35] which involved the reaction between -NH2 groups of aptamers and -COOH group on the surfaces of CDs and QDs to form amide bonds. CdTe/CdS/ZnS QDs were labelled with glucose aptamer, and CDs were labelled with insulin aptamer. The fluorescence of CDs and QDs was quenched by NG and then restored by respective analytes, i.e. insulin and glucose. The probe was optimized with respect to reaction parameters, which were concentrations of QDs, CDs, EDC, NG and aptamer, incubation temperature and time, reaction temperature and time, and the pH of the system.

QDs (1 mM, 212 μL) were mixed with EDC (1 mg/mL, 32 μL) and buffer solution (pH 7.4, 32 μL) and, the CDs (10 μg/mL, 50 μL) with EDC (1 mg/mL, 10 μL) and buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 μL). Both the mixtures were sonicated for 30 min at room temperature. Glucose aptamer (3 μM, 42 μL) was added to the activated QDs and insulin aptamer (3 μM, 40 μL) along with buffer solution (pH 7.4, 60 μL) was added to the activated CDs. Both mixtures were incubated at 25 °C for 2 h. Then, 32 μL of NG (0.45 mg/mL) was added to 318 μL of QDs-aptamer complex while 45 μL of NG (0.45 mg/mL) to 135 μL of CDs-aptamer complex, and both were incubated at 25 °C for 20 min. Then, 170 μL of the supernatant of quenched QDs’ mixture was drained off, and the remaining 180 μL cloudy layer was mixed with 180 μL of quenched CDs’ mixture using vortex mixer, and let the mixture at room temperature for 10 min to get a stable state. This QDs-CDs probe was used for the determination of glucose and insulin in human serum.

A reaction is controlled by various factors such as concentrations of ingredients, reaction temperature and pH etc. Maximum output of a reaction represents its optimum state where each parameter has its specific level. Some parameters may affect the reaction directly while others may affect in combination and there may be some factors which are essential components of the system but do not have a significant effect on the output. Different levels of reaction parameters were tried and, optimum levels were selected.

In case of this experiment, output was the restoration of fluorescence intensity of aptamer probe while reaction parameters included concentrations of QDs or CDs, EDC, aptamers, NG, incubation temperature and time, reaction temperature and time, and the pH. All parameters were varied and tested statistically using analysis of variance (ANOVA) with multiple comparisons. Moreover, incubation time indicated the time required for binding the aptamers to the surface of QDs or CDs while reaction time was the time required for the reaction between analyte, glucose or insulin, and the respective aptamer to restore luminescence of QDs or CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical Modeling: a multiple polynomial regression model
ce:section.ce:para: Aim to design the statistical model was to access the effects of multiple reaction parameters individually and/or in combination on the outcome, restoration of the FL intensity of QDs and CDs, using a model equation. Statistical modelling was finished in three steps. First step, parameters were predicted which had significant effect individually and/or in combination, on the response by formulating and testing the null hypothesis. Next step, an equation was designed which explained the variation of response with respect to reaction predictors. Finally, the designed model was tested by plotting actual values against predicted values (calculated using model equation), and the residual values against observed values and residual values versus predicted values. For the said purpose, determinant factors of the reaction were predicted by testing the following null and alternative hypothesis using ANOVA at a 5% level of significance. Null hypothesis (Ho): Reaction parameter (concentration of QDs or CDs, EDC, aptamer, NG, incubation temperature, incubation time, reaction temperature, reaction time or pH) did not significantly affect the FL intensity restoration, individually or/and in combination.

Alternative hypothesis (H1): Reaction parameter (concentration of QDs or CDs, EDC, aptamer, NG, incubation temperature, incubation time, reaction temperature, reaction time or pH) had a significant effect on FL intensity restoration, individually or/and in combination.

Profile plots of estimated marginal mean values of FL intensity restoration were drawn to identify the interaction effect of parameters in pairs on the response variable. The same result was further authenticated by p-values of the pairs of parameters calculated by using ANOVA, taking interaction effects under consideration.
Levene’s test was performed to check the equality of variance of FL intensity restoration among different parameters at a 5% significance level.
Five levels of each parameter were considered and hence a 45-run design was used to predict the model equation.

Human whole blood samples were provided by Yifu hospital Nanjing, an affiliated hospital of Nanjing Medical University, China. Blood specimens were placed at room temperature for 45 min in a centrifuge tube. After that, the samples were centrifuged at 2500 rpm for 15 min. The supernatants, i.e. the serums, were collected and stored at -40 °C before use.

50 μL of a serum sample was mixed with 360 μL of QDs-CDs probe, incubated at 30 °C for 1 h, and its fluorescence intensity was measured with FL spectrophotometer. The blank probe was prepared and processed in the same way by adding 50 μL of buffer (pH 7.4) instead of the serum sample. Fluorescence restoration was determined using the following equation.
(1) FLIT (%) = [(FS – Fb)/ Fb]*100

In Eq. (1), “FS” represents the fluorescence intensity of the probe with the sample while “Fb” means the fluorescence intensity of the blank probe. The concentration of glucose was determined using the straight-line equation while that of insulin was measured by the standard addition method. All samples were prepared and measured in triplicates.
McIlvaine buffer, 5 mM, was used in the experiment.

The probe was characterized for the confirmation of functional groups on the surface of CDs and QDs, and authentication of complex formation between the functional groups on nanoparticles and the respective aptamers.

Fig. 1 a presented FT-IR spectra of CDs and QDs where the salient peaks were at about 1000 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, 1600 cm−1, 2900 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1, and these peaks belonged to the OH bending/CC/CO stretching vibrations, symmetric vibrations of COO, asymmetric vibrations of COO, stretching vibrations of CH2 and stretching vibrations of OH, respectively [36,37]. These peaks indicated the presence of COO− and OH− groups on the surface of QDs and CDs.

To authenticate the complex formation, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were taken as shown in Fig. 1b and c, respectively. In Fig. 1b, a clear absorbance peak was emerged at about 260 nm in case of designed probe whereas no such peak was observed when the mixture of QDs and CDs were scanned in the wavelength range of 200–800 nm using UV–vis spectroscopy. The found peak is a characteristic peak of the aptamer, and the fact was an indication that aptamers had conjugated on the surfaces of CDs and QDs. The conjugation was further authenticated by Fig. 1c where a red shift was observed in FL spectra after complex formation. In Fig. 1c, FL peaks labelled as A and B belonged to CDs and QDs, respectively. Fig. 1d also verified the conjugation of aptamers. It is clear from Fig. 1d that aptamers complexed with CDs/QDs moved less distance on agarose gel compared to the pure aptamers and physical mixtures of aptamers and CDs/QDs. The glucose aptamer was of low molecular weight as compared to the insulin aptamer, and hence glucose aptamer-QDs travelled faster compared to the insulin aptamer-CDs resulting in glucose aptamer-QDs band closer to the bands formed by pure glucose aptamer and physical mixture of glucose aptamer and QDs.
Scheme 1 illustrated the design of the probe and its working principle to detect glucose and insulin, simultaneously.

It is clear from the Scheme 1 that various steps were involved in the development of probe and its principle. The surfaces of the fluorescence nano-particles, QDs and CDs, were firstly activated with EDC. The second step, the conjugation involved the reaction between activated COOH on the surface of nano-particles and –NH2 group attached to the 5’ end of the aptamers to make amide bonds. In this step, glucose aptamer was conjugated on QDs’ surface while insulin aptamer on CDs’ surface. Next step, FL quenching, involved the adsorption of QDs-aptamer/CDs-aptamer complexes on the surface of NG particles via π-π stacking [33].

Further studies were conducted to investigate the mechanism of quenching. It was found that an increase in temperature destabilized the fluorophore-quencher complex and resulted in a decrease in quenching effect, as shown in Fig. 2 a and b. In Fig. 2a and b, “Fo” and “F” denoted the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore, QDs or CDs, in the absence and presence of the quencher, NG. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of the NG and the FL spectrum of the aptamer-probe overlapped, Fig. 2c. The fluorescence lifetime measurements revealed no difference in the absence and presence of the quencher, i.e. Ʈo/Ʈ = 1, as shown in Fig. 2d. “Ʈo” and “Ʈ” denoted the lifetime of fluorophores, QDs and CDs, in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively. According to the literature, these findings indicated that the static quenching and the inner filter effect (IFE) were the possible mechanisms to quench the fluorescence of the QDs and the CDs by the NG [38,39].

Finally, the addition of glucose and insulin restored the fluorescence of CDs and QDs. In this step, glucose and insulin reacted with their respective aptamers and changed the conformation of aptamers. This alteration in aptamers resulted in halting the processes of static quenching and inner filter effect. Eventually, the fluorescence intensities of the fluorophores were restored.

Reaction parameters were optimized using systematic reaction optimization procedure, and ANOVA test with multiple comparisons was applied to thedetermination of the significant difference among different levels of the reaction conditions. Fig. S1 and S2 narrated the effects of different reaction parameters on the restoration of FL intensities (%) of QDs and CDs on the addition of glucose and insulin, respectively. The explanation of optimization facts was explained in supplementary data.

It was observed that all individual parameters of the experiment were best explained by non-linear polynomial functions. Then, the response was regressed on reaction parameters. ANOVA with main and interaction effects’ model was used to verify the fact of significance, i.e. whether parameters individually and/or in combination had a significant influence on FLIR or not. Fig. 3 explained the interaction effect of QDs’ concentration and incubation time.

In Fig. 3, y-axis was calibrated as estimated marginal mean values of FLIR (%), x-axis as the concentration of QDs while lines were plotted at five different incubation times. All the lines were in a parallel pattern which explained that there was no significant influence of the combined effect of QDs concentration and incubation temperature on the response. This description was further authenticated by the application of ANOVA with an interaction model that resulted in a p-value of 0.55 which was higher than our level of significance, 0.05. Hence, the null hypothesis was not rejected, i.e. there was no significant influence of the interaction effect of QDs concentration and incubation temperature on the response.

All the nine parameters were checked individually and in combination, 36 pairs, for significant influence on the outcome. All the p-values were <0.05 when checked individually but >0.05 when checked for interaction effects. Moreover, all the interaction plots presented parallel lines like Fig. 3. Hence, the null hypothesis about individual effects was rejected, i.e. there was no significant influence of individual parameters on the response and statement of null hypothesis about interaction effects was not rejected, i.e. there was no significant combined effect of parameters on the FLIR. A similar result appeared when CDs’ system of the aptasensor was analyzed.

In case of Levene’s test for both QDs’ and CDs’ systems, the p-value was >0.05 resulting in non-rejection of the null hypothesis, i.e. the variances of FL intensity restoration among different parameters ware equal.

It was concluded from the results of ANOVA that individual parameters significantly change the FLIR and the regression analysis resulted in a polynomial model which precisely described the effects of reaction parameters on FLIR. The general form of the predicted model was described by Eq. (2).
(2) FLIR (%) = βo + α14β1 4 + α13β1 3 + α12β1 2 + α11β1 + α24β2 4 + α23β2 3 + α22β2 2 + α21β2 + α34β3 4 + α33β3 3 + α32β3 2 + α31β3 + α44β4 4 + α43β4 3 + α42β4 2 + α41β4 + α54β5 4 + α53β5 3 + α52β5 2 + α51β5 + α63β6 3 + α62β6 2 + α61β6 + α74β7 4 + α73β7 3 + α72β7 2 + α71β7 + α84β8 4 + α83β8 3 + α82β8 2 + α81β8 + α94β9 4 + α93β9 3 + α92β9 2 + α91β9
In Eq. (2), βo = intercept, β1 = Conc. of QDs (mM) or CDs (μg/mL), β2 = NG Conc. (μg/mL), β3 = aptamer Conc. (μM), β4 = EDC Conc. (mg/mL), β5 = Incubation Temperature (oC), β6 = Incubation Time (min), β7 = Reaction Temperature (oC), β8 = Reaction Time (min), β9 = pH while α-values represent the regression coefficients of the related polynomial terms. The predicted response was denoted by FLIR (%).
Model was verified by Observed, predicted and standard residual plots as shown in Fig. 4 .

Fig. 4 explained the plots between different variables for authentication of the model equation. The variables included were the observed values (actual values), predicted values (values predicted by the model equation) and standard residual values of QDs’ FLI restoration %. The plots of predicted versus observed values were the straight lines with gradual increments whiles plots of standard residuals versus observed values and standard residuals versus predicted values were almost straight lines parallel to the x-axis or y-axis. These plots were the validity of the fact that the predicted model equation was best explaining the effects of reaction parameters on the outcome and can be used to predict the value of FLI restoration %, the outcome, at specific levels of reaction determinants.
The analysis of the model equation related to FLI restoration % of CDs resulted in similar observed, predicted and standard residual plots as were observed in the QDs’ system.
The probe was validated in terms of the determination of the linear range, sensitivity, reproducibility and robustness.

Fig. S3 explained the linear relationship between (Fs-Fb)/Fb and concentration of analytes. The linear relationship between response and the concentration was noticed when the concentration range of insulin was 0.2–2 nM, and glucose was 0.5–7 mM. The line equation for insulin was y = 0.3311x + 0.0044 with R2 of 0.9998 while for glucose it was y = 0.0808x + 0.0008 with R2 of 0.9999. The normal human serum insulin level is in nano-molar range while normal serum glucose concentration is in milli-molar range. Hence, both of the resulted linear ranges were suitable for the simultaneous determination of serum glucose and insulin levels.

Fig. 5 showed the recovery of FLI of the aptamer-probe quenched with NG when concentrations of insulin and glucose were varied. FLI peaks labelled as “A” with emission wavelengths of 450 nm, were related to the CDs while peaks “B” with emission wavelengths of 585 nm, were of the QDs. It is clear from Fig. 5 that an increase in the concentration of insulin and glucose caused a proportional increase in FLI restoration.

The limit of detection (LOD) was determined by using “3σ/k”. LOD for insulin was “0.018 nM” while for glucose it was “0.058 mM”. These values indicate that the aptasensor has enough sensitivity for the analysis of said biomolecules in human serum.
The reproducibility was determined by measuring intraday and interday precision. Table 1 summarizes the results of accuracy and precision for insulin and glucose.

All the values of relative errors (RE) were with the range of −5.00–4.00% and the values of relative standard deviations (RSD) were ≤3.77%. These results narrated that the designed method was reproducible and robust with respect to precision and accuracy. Hence, it can be used for clinical diagnosis.

To verify the selectivity of the aptasensor, various interfering species were added to the probe in the presence of analytes. Blood is a complex matrix, and it is hard to check the interference of every constituent of the blood. However, common ions and components that may affect the efficiency of analytical methods can be considered as interfering agents. Table 2 described the interfering agents used in the experiment, their concentrations and relative FLI difference for the aptamer-probe with and without the addition of interfering species while in the presence of glucose and insulin.

It is clear from Table 2 that variations in FLIR% in case of all the interfering agents were less than 5%. Moreover, the concentrations of interfering species used were much higher than the maximum level found in blood. The results supported the specificity of the method for the analytes of interest.

To prove the suitability for real sample analysis, the designed aptasensor was used to determine the insulin and glucose levels in human serum. The serums of three healthy volunteers were analyzed. The glucose level was measured directly using the straight line equation for glucose while the standard addition method was used to determine insulin concentration, as the insulin level of human serum is very low. For the said design, standard human insulin solution at five different levels but within the linear range was added to the serum samples before analysis. Then, a graph of spiked insulin concentration verses (Fs-Fb)/Fb was plotted for each serum sample. The actual concentration of the insulin in serum was found by extrapolation of the graph, and it referred to the value where the plot intersected the concentration axis. If detected level of the analyte is found closer or equal to the upper level of linear range, serum can be diluted with the buffer of pH 7.4, and the actual result can be found by multiplying the obtained result with dilution factor. Table 3 presented the RSD and recoveries of insulin while RSDs of glucose along with the detected insulin and glucose levels in serum samples of healthy volunteers.

The results in Table 3 showed that all recoveries of insulin were within the range of 95–101% while all RSDs of the insulin as well as glucose were <5%. This was an indication of the high reproducibility of the method.

The accuracy of the analytical method was further verified by measuring the serum insulin and glucose levels using the designed probe. Furthermore, the accuracy was authenticated by comparing the results of the current method with the values obtained from the clinical laboratory of the Yifu hospital Nanjing, an affiliated hospital of the Nanjing Medical University China, at the same time frame and same serum samples of healthy human volunteers. The gold standard methods were used at the laboratory of the hospital. The results were summarized in Table 4 .

It was clear from the results summarized in Table 4 that the recoveries % of the designed probe in comparison to the gold standard protocols were within the range of 90–110% which indicated the clinical diagnostic significance of the probe for applications in real sample analysis.
Table 5 described some specific features of the dual emissive probe in comparison with the reported methods.

The features specified in Table 5 indicated that the dual emissive probe had low cost per unit test in comparison to the already reported methods (the cost was estimated based on the cost of materials used as aptamers are much cheaper than antibodies and enzymes), suitable accuracy, precision, selectivity, sensitivity and linear ranges as well as had appropriate recovery when used for detection of said biomolecules in human serum which is a complex matrix.

Our research group designed a fluorescence-based dual emissive aptamer probe for simultaneous detection of glucose and insulin in human serum for the very first time. Moreover, glucose aptamer was not used before for the said purpose.

Briefly, a dual emissive nano aptasensor, based on the static quenching and the inner filter effect of CdTe/CdS/ZnS QDs and CDs, was successfully designed to determine the concentration of glucose and insulin in human serum simultaneously. All reaction parameters were optimized using ANOVA, and a polynomial regression model was constructed which explained the effects of reaction parameters on the restoration of fluorescence in an efficient way. The designed statistical model was verified by observed, predicted and residual plots. The model was proved a valuable tool to access the effects of multiple variables, at their specific levels, on a single factor using a model equation. Moreover, the probe was validated, and it was concluded that it was sensitive with suitable linear ranges, specific and robust with reproducible results. Furthermore, aptasensor proved efficient and cost-effective when used to determine serum glucose and insulin levels of healthy volunteers. Hence, designed aptasensor can be used in clinical diagnostic laboratories to diagnose diabetes and its type with a single assay.
There is no conflict of interest in any part of this research project or any section of this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.61775099, 21705080) and Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No.BK20171487, BK20171043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.04.119.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
